1: Woe to those who enact unjust laws. The woe-oracle (hoy) opens chapter 10 with a direct address to the ruling class. The Hebrew chaqaq (enact/decree) is a technical legal term for official legislation — the critique is not of individual wrongdoing but of institutionalized injustice built into the legal system itself. This is system-level corruption, not personal sin alone.
2: Turning the poor away from justice and robbing the rights of the afflicted. Mishpat (justice/legal right) is the legal entitlement that the poor have by covenant law (Deut 24-25; Exod 22-23). The MKT's rights is strong — the poor have legal claims being actively suppressed. The oppressor is not merely neglecting the poor but perverting the legal system to strip them of what the Torah guarantees.
3: The day of reckoning (yom pekudah) — the day of visitation, inspection, or accounting. The MKT renders it day of reckoning, capturing the auditing metaphor: on that day the books will be opened and the corrupt rulers will have nowhere to put their unjustly acquired wealth. The rhetorical question — where will you flee? — parallels the Day of the LORD language of Amos 5:18-20.
4: Nothing left but to cower among captives or collapse among the dead. Two parallel fates: imprisoned or dead. The Hebrew presents the impossibility of escape from divine judgment. The refrain closes again: his hand is stretched out still — the fourth and final occurrence, showing that the woe-oracle against social injustice is the same divine hand at work as in the covenant judgments of chapter 9.
5: Woe, Assyria — you are the rod of my anger; the club in whose hand is my fury! A stunning reversal: Assyria, the most feared empire of the ancient Near East, is merely a tool in YHWH's hand. The matteh (rod/staff) and maqqel (club/stick) are implements — the metaphor fully worked out in 10:15 (the axe does not boast over the woodcutter). The MKT's rod of my anger and club of my fury maintain the double-object structure of the Hebrew.
6: The LORD sends Assyria against a godless nation — Isaiah's own people. The Hebrew chaneph (godless/profane) is the same word used for those who have desecrated themselves. The covenant people are called godless for their covenant infidelity — a prophetic indictment that would have been as shocking to Isaiah's audience as Paul's declaration that Jews as well as Gentiles are under sin (Rom 3:9).
7: That is not his intention. The Assyrian king does not know he is fulfilling divine purposes — he acts from imperial ambition, not divine calling. This establishes the principle of secondary causation: human actors pursue their own goals while unknowingly fulfilling God's providential purposes. The NT applies this to the crucifixion (Acts 4:27-28) — the rulers acted from their own motives while fulfilling what God had predestined.
8: Are not all my officers kings? The Assyrian boast of imperial hierarchy — officers who are equivalent to kings in their power — illustrates the pride the MKT is about to deconstruct. The word melech (king) is flagged as dispute-level 2; in this context it functions as a superlative: my officers are as powerful as other nations' kings, implying Assyria's officers outrank everyone else's rulers.
9: The list of conquered cities — Calno, Carchemish, Hamath, Arpad, Samaria, Damascus — is a catalogue of imperial conquests cited as precedents for the inevitable fall of Jerusalem. The rhetorical move is boastful induction: if all these have fallen, Jerusalem will too. Isaiah's point is that the boast contains its own refutation — Assyria's confidence in its track record is precisely the pride that will precede its fall.
10: Just as my hand reached kingdoms whose idols surpassed those of Jerusalem. The Assyrian king's idolatrous logic: if I could defeat nations with better idols, I can certainly defeat Jerusalem. He evaluates divine power by the quality of idol craftsmanship — the very category mistake Isaiah's idol polemic (44:9-20) deconstructs. The LORD is not an idol whose power can be assessed by comparison; he is the living God who raises up and tears down empires.
11: Shall I not treat Jerusalem and her idols just as I treated Samaria and her carved images? The Assyrian king's symmetry argument — Samaria fell; Jerusalem will fall by the same logic. The inclusion of idols in Jerusalem is historically accurate: under Ahaz and Manasseh, idolatry was practiced in the temple. Isaiah's response (v. 12) is that once YHWH has used Assyria, he will deal with Assyria's own arrogance.
12: Once the Lord has completed his work on Mount Zion. The melech (king) of Assyria is flagged — in context the proud heart and haughty eyes of the king. The fruit of the arrogant heart of the king of Assyria — the MKT renders this as the product/result of pride, making the king's internal attitude the target of divine judgment. The word kavod (glory/honor) is flagged in the surrounding verses — Assyria's glory is exactly what will be consumed (vv. 16-18).
13: It is by my own power and wisdom that I have done this. The Assyrian king's speech (vv. 13-14) is a masterpiece of self-aggrandizement: I erased boundaries, plundered treasuries, subdued populations as easily as taking eggs from an abandoned nest. The MKT preserves the first-person singulars throughout, which intensifies the arrogance: every verb is I did. The accumulation sets up the devastating axe-metaphor of v. 15.
14: No one fluttered a wing, or opened a beak, or chirped. The deserted nest metaphor — eggs taken without any resistance — is one of Isaiah's most vivid images of imperial ease. The three negatives (no wing-flutter, no beak-open, no chirp) emphasize total passivity of the conquered. The MKT preserves the avian vocabulary, which is essential for the rhetorical effect; scooped up riches captures the casual reach of empire.
15: Does an axe boast over the person who wields it? The tool-instrument metaphor: four instruments (axe, saw, rod, staff) and their absurdity if they claim agency over the person using them. The kavod (glory) in v. 16 is flagged — the Sovereign LORD will send a wasting disease on Assyria's stout warriors and will kindle a blazing fire under Assyria's glory. The MKT's glory captures the kabod's range from physical splendor to proud status.
16: A wasting disease among the Assyrian's stout warriors. The Hebrew mishmanav (stout ones/fat ones) is a description of the elite warrior class, well-fed and formidable. Kavod is flagged — rendered glory. Under the glory there will be kindled a fire like a burning flame. The image is of the brilliant exterior concealing the consuming fire that will hollow it out from within — divine judgment working from the inside.
17: The Light of Israel will become a fire, and his Holy One a flame. The divine titles here are striking: the LORD is Israel's Light (a positive presence-title, Ps 27:1) who becomes a consuming fire against Assyria (a judgment-title, Heb 12:29). Both attributes belong to the same God. The MKT preserves both titles without merging them into a generic deity. The fire consumes briers and thorns first — the worthless growth — then the great forest.
18: It will consume the glory of his forest. The kavod (glory) and nefesh (soul/life) are both flagged here. Nefesh in this context means the whole vitality — the MKT's both root and branch is a good rendering though it paraphrases. It will be as when a sick man wastes away — the Hebrew mesos nasas is a medical-deterioration image: the empire will not fall suddenly in battle but will waste away like a terminal illness.
19: So few that a child could write them down. From the dense impenetrable forest (the Assyrian army's metaphor) to a handful of trees a child can enumerate — total decimation described through the reversal of the forest imagery. The hyperbole (child counting) emphasizes the completeness of the reduction. This anticipates the remnant theology of 10:20-22: a small number surviving a massive catastrophe is both judgment and grace.
20: In that day the remnant of Israel will no longer rely on him who struck them. Emet (truth/faithfulness) is flagged — rendered in truth. The remnant will rely on the LORD in truth — in faithfulness, in genuine covenant loyalty rather than the formal religion that has characterized Judah. The turn from relying on Assyria (the instrument of discipline) to relying on the LORD who disciplines is the model of true conversion.
21: A remnant will return — the remnant of Jacob — to the Mighty God. El Gibbor (Mighty God) appears here — the same title used for the Messiah in 9:6. The remnant returns to the same Mighty God who is the Messiah's title, creating a theological echo: the one who rules on David's throne (9:7) and the one to whom the remnant returns (10:21) are identified by the same divine title.
22: Only a remnant will return. Tsedaqah is flagged — the decreed destruction is in righteousness. Paul cites this verse in Rom 9:27 as evidence that even in the OT, mere physical descent from Israel did not guarantee inclusion in the people of God — only the remnant is saved. The tension between numerical abundance (sand of the sea) and covenantal reality (only a remnant) is foundational for Paul's theology of election.
23: The Lord GOD of hosts is executing his decreed destruction throughout the whole land. The double title Adonai YHWH-Sabaoth emphasizes the triple sovereignty: master, covenant God, commander of armies. The destruction is decreed (kalah ve-necheretset) — cut, determined, inscribed in the divine plan. The MKT's his decreed destruction captures the irrevocability; this is not contingent judgment but fulfilled inevitability.
24: O my people who live in Zion, do not be afraid of the Assyrians. The same divine voice that decrees destruction now addresses his people with comfort — the same tension as the double edge of the outstretched hand. The divine name YHWH-Sabaoth addresses Zion as my people — the intimate covenant address in the midst of the judgment speech. The rod will be lifted, as at Midian (v. 26).
25: For in a very little while my fury will be spent. The temporariness of divine anger is a key Isaianic theme — anger is an instrument, not an attribute. The wrath against Judah passes through Assyria to expire; then the wrath turns against Assyria's arrogance. This double movement — through instrument to target — structures much of chapters 7-37: the LORD uses and then judges the nations he uses.
26: The LORD of hosts will lash out against them as he defeated Midian at the rock of Oreb. The divine name YHWH-Sabaoth is flagged. The Midian defeat (Gideon, Judg 7-8) and the Egyptian crossing are both cited as precedents for miraculous divine deliverance against impossible odds — the new Exodus typology that runs through Isaiah 40-55 begins here in chapter 10.
27: The yoke will be lifted from your shoulders because of the anointing. The Hebrew lifney-shamen is disputed: because of fatness (the yoke breaks because the ox's neck is too fat) or because of anointing oil (the Davidic anointing breaks the Assyrian yoke). The MKT's because of the anointing reads the messianic option, which connects to the anointed ruler of 9:6-7 and 11:1-5 — a defensible interpretation that makes the liberation messianic.
28: Verses 28-32 are a dramatic march of an advancing army through Benjaminite territory toward Jerusalem — town by town, with trembling spreading as the army advances. The place-names are listed not for geographical instruction but for dramatic effect: the reader feels the inexorable advance through familiar places. The MKT preserves the place-name list without paraphrase, respecting the dramatic structure.
29: At Geba they camp for the night; Ramah shakes with fear; Gibeah of Saul flees. The shaking and fleeing of these towns creates a wave of terror that anticipates the final image at Nob. Each town mentioned is within sight of Jerusalem — the proximity is the terror. The MKT's active verbs (shakes, flees) are direct; some versions soften these to past tense narration, losing the dramatic immediacy.
30: Cry out loudly, O daughter of Gallim! Listen, O Laishah! The feminine address — daughter of Gallim — is a personification device common in the prophets (daughter of Babylon, daughter of Zion). The call to listen is urgent; the towns are being called to witness what is coming toward them, perhaps even to warn each other. The MKT's imperative forms preserve the urgency.
31: Two more towns: Madmenah flees, Gebim's people scatter for cover. The escalating panic as the army approaches is Isaiah's way of making the reader feel the terror viscerally. The brevity of these verses — almost pure action verbs — mimics the speed and chaos of flight before an advancing army.
32: That same day he will halt at Nob and shake his fist toward the hill of Zion. Nob is within sight of Jerusalem — the military climax of the march. The gesture of shaking the fist is one of taunting threat, not action. At this precise moment (the next verse) the divine hand intervenes. The juxtaposition is dramatic: the Assyrian halts, threatens with a fist — and then the LORD lops off the branches with terrifying power.
33: The Sovereign LORD of hosts is about to lop off the branches with terrifying power. The divine name YHWH-Sabaoth is flagged. The divine Lumberjack suddenly appears at the moment of the Assyrian's triumph. The tall trees (gavohim) fall — the imagery of the great forest being cut down will become the stump of Jesse in 11:1. Judgment clears the forest so a new shoot can grow.
34: He will slash down the dense forest with iron, and Lebanon will fall before a powerful one. The chapter ends with the forest cleared — the imperial forest of Assyria reduced to stumps. This clears the narrative space for the next chapter's entirely different image: not a dense threatening forest but a single tiny shoot (choter) emerging from a stump. The contrast between 10:34 and 11:1 is one of Isaiah's most deliberate structural juxtapositions.
